frameworks.name: decision learning models
frameworks.one_line: Trial-by-trial models combining utility, learning, and choice rules for gambling tasks.

frameworks.name: prospect utility function
frameworks.one_line: Nonlinear utility shape highlighted as enabling generalizable predictions.

frameworks.name: generalization criterion method
frameworks.one_line: Evaluating models by a priori prediction across tasks, not within-task fit.

core_question: Which decision learning models generalize parameters estimated on one gambling task to predict choice on another?
key_assumptions: Same participant's parameters transfer across structurally different gambling tasks
A priori prediction is a stronger model test than post hoc fit
Short- and long-term predictions may require different learning machinery
manipulations: Task identity (Iowa Gambling Task vs Soochow Gambling Task)
Evaluation criterion (post hoc fit vs short-term vs long-term generalization)
Model family across 8 candidate decision learning models
key_findings: Models with a prospect utility function produced generalizable cross-task predictions
Best model for short-term prediction differed from best model for long-term prediction
Post hoc fit ranking did not match generalization ranking
interpretations: Prospect-style utility is the component that travels across simple gambling tasks
Distinct learning processes may govern short- versus long-horizon choice
